title: Binaries for Debian / Ubuntu
body:
### Feature request description

It would be great if up to date binaries were stored in an official repository for podman.

Docker does this and it's very easy to install on any major Linux distribution. Podman should do that same so that users aren't stuck on very outdated releases of podman.

### Suggest potential solution

Host a repository for binaries for major Linux distributions like Debian / Ubuntu.

Please build for arm64 too as this is more popular in the server market.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

I've built from source before, but it's a giant pain in the ass having to manually build everything and then copy all of the files to multiple machines.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
Please see https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/25219, https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/17362 and many more discussions and issue son this repo.

Are you volunteering to create and maintain such repo?

Or https://www.reddit.com/r/podman/comments/1impu1l/updating_podman_on_ubuntu_2404/ or https://www.reddit.com/r/podman/comments/1in6yjh/quadlets_on_ubuntu_which_version/ if Reddit is your thing.

Debian and Ubuntu ship perfectly up to date packages, as well as old ones. Podman is perfectly integrated in those distributions **as designed**.

@sbrivio-rh I don't think that the guidance to use Ubuntu 25.04 is a realistic one for many people. For example, the latest GitHub Actions runner Ubuntu image is 24.04 and there isn't 25.04 planned. So anyone attempting to use podman in their GHA CI setups is stuck with podman 4.9.3. Folks in many cases cannot freely pick the base OS version.

> [@sbrivio-rh](https://github.com/sbrivio-rh) I don't think that the guidance to use Ubuntu 25.04 is a realistic one for many people. For example, the latest GitHub Actions runner Ubuntu image is 24.04 and there isn't 25.04 planned.

Well but that looks like an issue with GitHub, right? I mean, that's not an issue with Debian, Ubuntu, Podman... anything we can help with.

I'm not sure I'd call it an issue -- if Ubuntu doesn't rebase within release, that's simply the policy of that release. OTOH, podman has seen quite fast development still, and might want to provide an easy way for getting it into developers' / users' hands.

For the specific GitHub situation, the podman project could create and maintain a GitHub Action action that people could use with
```
  steps:
    - uses: containers/podman-install-latest@v1
```
or something similar. It would then install package / binary from the latest `containers/podman` release artifacts, which could also be built automatically for each new release.
That is doable, but who is going to do that and especially maintain it?  In the long term, that seems more fragile than just getting a new Podman in Ubuntu (or change distro when possible)

> I'm not sure I'd call it an issue -- if Ubuntu doesn't rebase within release, that's simply the policy of that release.

Of the distribution, rather.

But sure, I wasn't pointing at an issue with Ubuntu. I was rather pointing at an issue with proprietary software, or the fact that for whatever reason you can't choose the version of the distribution you want.

> For the specific GitHub situation, the podman project could create and maintain a GitHub Action action

...if I understood correctly, that would still need an out-of-distribution package, right?

Because that would be the most problematic part I think (regardless of the fact that Docker does that). Even letting alone the maintenance burden, LSM policies become pointless, and users get issues from integrations that were never tested. I'd call it a disservice to the ecosystem.

> In the long term, that seems more fragile than just getting a new Podman in Ubuntu

Note that there are pretty recent packages in Ubuntu (5.4.1), that's not (anymore?) the topic here.

unfortunately, github actions will stay on 24.04 until the next LTS in 2026. it would be extremely helpful to demonstrate an example how to upgrade to latest/5x version of podman. it's a common enough use-case i think.
